topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of damp earth and aged bark hit me first, a familiar comfort that suddenly curdled in my throat. I stood at the edge of the clearing, my shadow stretching long and accusing in the late afternoon sun. Where the towering oak, my childhood monument, had stood just last summer, there was now only a jagged scar on the hillside, splintered wood littering the disturbed soil. My hands clenched into fists so tight my knuckles bleached white. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and brittle. I walked toward the stump, my boots crunching on the loose debris. Every fallen branch felt like a personal insult, a deliberate vandalism against my memory. I kicked at the base of the remaining trunk, a sharp, frustrated sound escaping my chest. This wasn't just a tree; it was a promise, a silent pact I had made with the ground, and someone had just violated it.

[story 2]

I found the evidence near the creek bed—a scattering of freshly cut limbs, too clean, too deliberate. My breath hitched, catching painfully in my lungs. It was the same spot. The spot where I, at ten years old, had painstakingly nurtured that sapling, watering it with stolen coins and whispering encouragement to it grow strong. Now, the space was hollowed out, raw earth exposed like an open wound. A hot, tight pressure built behind my eyes. I didn't cry; tears felt too small for the magnitude of the betrayal. I paced back and forth, my movements jerky and agitated. "Who authorized this?" I demanded, my voice pitched high and vibrating with a furious disbelief. The silence of the woods seemed to amplify the sheer audacity of the act, making my jaw lock so hard I could feel the strain in my molars.

[story 3]

It took me nearly an hour to process the sight. The sheer scale of the devastation made my stomach churn. I knelt down, brushing aside dry leaves to see the exposed roots, the place where I had first felt a connection to something enduring. Then I saw it—the clear evidence of recent violence: sawed-off ends, the unmistakable mark of a heavy-duty saw. A deep, cold anger settled over me, chilling me despite the summer warmth. I rose slowly, deliberately, gathering the pieces of broken wood. My gaze swept over the empty space, lingering on the ragged edge of the cut. I didn't shout; instead, a low, dangerous growl rumbled deep in my chest. "You think this is acceptable?" I challenged the empty air, my voice dangerously level, every syllable laced with a profound sense of wrongness. I turned abruptly and walked away, not running, but moving with a stiff, purposeful stride, leaving the scene of destruction behind me.
